metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC bullet 1 / AC bullet 4) that explicitly verifies the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar is present in `docker-compose.yml` with a read-only Docker socket mount, and that `docker compose config` confirms its service definition.
findings.finding: The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar service is described as a deliverable in the Files section ('add … + the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (Docker socket `:ro`)') but no acceptance criterion verifies that it was added, configured correctly, or tested.
findings.location: Files section — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` edit; Acceptance Criteria bullet 1 and bullet 4 (Tests)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC bullet 1 to read: '…+ the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar added to `docker-compose.yml` with `volumes: /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro`.' Add a test line in AC bullet 4: '`docker compose config` output includes the `autoheal` service with the `:ro` socket mount.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `:ro` from the Docker socket mount for the autoheal sidecar — `willfarrell/autoheal` requires write access to `/var/run/docker.sock` to call the Docker restart API; change the mount to `/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (no `:ro`).
findings.finding: The plan mounts the Docker socket for the autoheal sidecar as `:ro` (read-only), but `willfarrell/autoheal` must issue `POST /containers/{id}/restart` Docker API calls to restart unhealthy containers — a write operation that a read-only socket mount physically prevents. This makes the autoheal restart mechanism non-functional as specified.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`: 'the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (Docker socket `:ro`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In docker-compose.yml, declare the autoheal service socket mount as `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (rw, the default). If a security policy requires limiting autoheal's surface, use Docker socket proxy (e.g. `tecnativa/docker-socket-proxy`) scoped to `POST /containers/*/restart` — but a plain `:ro` mount cannot work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Docker socket mount mode for the autoheal sidecar from `:ro` to `:rw` (required for restarts) AND add an explicit access-posture declaration in the compose file comment — state that the socket grant is write-scoped to container restart only, justify it as the minimum necessary for autoheal, and note the host-privilege implication (OWASP A05 / T5c least-privilege).
findings.finding: The plan declares the Docker socket for the willfarrell/autoheal sidecar as ':ro', but autoheal must POST /containers/{id}/restart (a write) to perform its function — ':ro' silently disables restarts. If corrected to ':rw' (the standard and required mount), the actual access posture (host-root-equivalent write access to the Docker daemon) is broader than declared and the plan leaves it undeclared/unjustified.
findings.location: Files section — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` EDIT: 'the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (Docker socket `:ro`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `docker-compose.yml`, mount the socket as `/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (`:rw`, the autoheal default) and add a comment such as: `# Docker socket :rw required — autoheal POSTs /containers/{id}/restart; this is the minimum grant for autoheal and is the accepted host-trust boundary for this single-tenant box (ADR-XXXX or inline rationale).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the flock test, e.g.: `docker run -d --name al1 -v autolander-state:/state autolander && docker run --rm -v autolander-state:/state autolander; [ $? -ne 0 ] && echo PASS` (assert non-zero exit from the second instance).
findings.finding: The flock second-instance test has no concrete proving command — AC bullet 3 states 'a second container instance exits on the flock' but provides no script, docker run invocation, or CI step that would produce evidence of this behavior. An executor has no specified way to demonstrate the guard works.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — bullet 3 ('Tests: … a second container instance exits on the flock')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Tests AC: 'Run `docker run -d --name al1 -v autolander-state:/state autolander && sleep 1 && docker run --rm -v autolander-state:/state autolander; echo "exit:$?"` — assert exit code is non-zero (flock refused). Tear down with `docker rm -f al1`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name the autolander SITE_SUBDIRS entry and the corresponding `external: true` volume in the plan (e.g. `autolander` → `gerrit_autolander`) so the two-file naming contract driven by `volume_for_subdir` is pinned and verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that adding the autolander state volume to SITE_SUBDIRS in compose-up.sh is sufficient for the config-check.sh drift gate to pass — but never names the autolander volume or its SITE_SUBDIRS entry. The volume_for_subdir function (compose-up.sh:35) derives names as `gerrit_<subdir>` bound to `/var/gerrit/site/<subdir>`. If the implementer chooses a subdir name (e.g. `autolander`) the derived volume is `gerrit_autolander`, and docker-compose.yml must declare exactly that name as `external: true`. Without pinning the name in the plan, this two-file naming contract is unverifiable and a mismatch (e.g. `autolander_state` in compose vs `gerrit_autolander` from SITE_SUBDIRS) would make check 5 fail at deploy time.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` / ## Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (State volume)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Add `autolander` to SITE_SUBDIRS; the derived volume name is `gerrit_autolander` (via `volume_for_subdir`); declare `gerrit_autolander: external: true` in docker-compose.yml.' This pins the contract and makes the drift gate trivially checkable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete SITE_SUBDIRS token and the derived Docker volume name (e.g. token `autolander` → volume `autolander_state` or `gerrit_autolander`) in the plan so all three files (Dockerfile mount path, docker-compose.yml volumes key, compose-up.sh SITE_SUBDIRS) are unambiguously aligned.
findings.finding: The actual Docker volume name string for the autolander state volume is never stated in the plan — it is referred to only as 'the autolander named volume' and 'autolander state volume'. Three separate artifacts (docker-compose.yml volumes stanza, compose-up.sh SITE_SUBDIRS token, and the flock path inside the container) must agree on the same derived string, and the derivation rule in compose-up.sh (gerrit_ prefix + hyphens→underscores) means the token added to SITE_SUBDIRS determines the volume name. Without naming the token, the plan under-specifies the edit.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` EDIT; ## Acceptance Criteria — State volume AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section or AC: 'SITE_SUBDIRS token: `autolander`; derived volume name: `autolander_state` (note: does NOT carry the `gerrit_` prefix used by Gerrit site volumes — declare it without that prefix in docker-compose.yml and use `volume_for_subdir autolander` in compose-up.sh)' OR if following the existing gerrit_ pattern, state 'token: `autolander`, volume: `gerrit_autolander`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical volume name (e.g. `autolander-state`) explicitly in the plan so it can be used verbatim in docker-compose.yml, SITE_SUBDIRS, and the in-container state path.
findings.finding: The named volume identifier is never stated in the plan — it is referenced as 'the autolander named volume' and 'autolander state volume' throughout, but the actual string (e.g. `autolander-state` or `autolander_data`) that must appear consistently in docker-compose.yml `volumes:`, `compose-up.sh` SITE_SUBDIRS, and the in-container flock/heartbeat path is left for the executor to invent. A mismatch across these three locations would silently break the drift gate or the flock guard.
findings.location: Files section + AC bullet 3 ('autolander named volume … added to SITE_SUBDIRS')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The volume is named `autolander-state` throughout — use this exact string in the compose `volumes:` stanza, `SITE_SUBDIRS`, and the container's `/state` mount path.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin `willfarrell/autoheal` to a specific version tag or image digest in the plan, and verify that the pinned version supports both `:ro` Docker socket access and `autoheal=true` label-based restart.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `willfarrell/autoheal` works with a `:ro` Docker socket mount and label-based restart (`autoheal=true`) but specifies no image version or digest. The image's label-based targeting behavior and read-only socket compatibility are version-dependent capabilities that are unverified for any specific pinned version.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` ('willfarrell/autoheal sidecar (Docker socket `:ro`)')
findings.suggested_fix: In the docker-compose.yml snippet for the autoheal sidecar, use a pinned tag such as `willfarrell/autoheal:v0.9.0` (or a SHA digest) and cite the release notes confirming `:ro` socket and label-based restart are supported at that version.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar belongs in S5a (where HEALTHCHECK and autoheal=true are both absent) or should be deferred to S5d alongside the HEALTHCHECK it requires to function, given that `restart: unless-stopped` already handles crash-restarts for the existing services.
findings.finding: The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar is wired into this plan before its only operative dependency (HEALTHCHECK) and its only AC-level consumer (autoheal=true label) exist — both are explicitly deferred to S5d. Without a HEALTHCHECK, Docker reports no health status and autoheal never triggers; restart: unless-stopped (already present on both existing services) already handles crash-restart. Introducing the sidecar here — a pattern-copy of the autoheal+HEALTHCHECK combo — is cargo-cult: the mechanism is split across stories and non-functional in this unit. Rule-of-three: zero existing autoheal call-sites in the codebase.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` entry; AC line 1 ('autolander compose service')
findings.suggested_fix: Move the autoheal sidecar addition to S5d, co-located with the HEALTHCHECK that makes it functional. This story (S5a) needs only the autolander service with restart: unless-stopped (matching the existing services) and the flock guard — the autoheal+HEALTHCHECK combo can land atomically in S5d when both halves are present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar and `autoheal=true` label are deliverables of S5a or S5d, and explicitly note that the sidecar is inert until S5d adds the HEALTHCHECK (or move the sidecar addition to S5d).
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar is added here and that the autolander is labeled `autoheal=true` 'per S5d' — but autoheal only restarts containers that report an `unhealthy` Docker health status, which requires a HEALTHCHECK instruction. The plan explicitly defers HEALTHCHECK to S5d. The plan's premise that the autoheal sidecar added in S5a will function as a restart mechanism is therefore unbacked: without a HEALTHCHECK, autoheal cannot act, so the sidecar is inert for the entire S5a→S5d window. This is not merely a sequencing note — it is an implicit assertion that the sidecar deployment here is meaningful infrastructure, which the absence of HEALTHCHECK refutes.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` (autoheal sidecar) / `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` ('HEALTHCHECK added by S5d')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either defer the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar addition to S5d (where the HEALTHCHECK that drives it is also defined) so both land atomically, OR explicitly document in the S5a plan that autoheal is intentionally inert until S5d and add an AC note confirming this transient state is acceptable.
findings.finding: The autoheal sidecar is added in S5a (docker-compose.yml edit) but the HEALTHCHECK it depends on is explicitly deferred to S5d. Between S5a and S5d landing, the autoheal container is deployed and running but will never trigger a restart (no HEALTHCHECK = Docker reports the container as 'healthy' by default, so autoheal never fires). The plan does not document this as an accepted transient state, nor does it consider holding the autoheal sidecar back to S5d where the HEALTHCHECK is also defined — shipping both atomically would eliminate a meaningless intermediate infra state where the sidecar consumes Docker socket access and resources with zero functional benefit.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` EDIT; AC bullet 'labeled `autoheal=true`, per S5d'; Dockerfile note '`HEALTHCHECK` added by S5d'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar stanza from S5a's docker-compose.yml edit into S5d's scope, alongside the HEALTHCHECK. S5a only needs: the autolander service definition, the flock entrypoint, the external volume declaration, and the SITE_SUBDIRS extension. The sidecar + label `autoheal=true` can be a single atomic addition in S5d.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as an edit target (or explicitly acknowledge the autolander service is outside autodeploy's v1 auto-apply surface): add `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` to `BOT_PATHS` and extend the deploy block to include `autolander` in the `docker compose up -d` call, OR document the intentional scope boundary.
findings.finding: autodeploy.sh is an unenumerated consumer of both docker-compose.yml and Dockerfile.autolander: (a) BOT_PATHS already includes docker-compose.yml, so adding autolander+autoheal services there causes autodeploy to trigger a review-bot rebuild/restart — but autodeploy only runs `docker compose up -d review-bot`, never bringing up the new autolander or autoheal services; (b) Dockerfile.autolander is not in BOT_PATHS, so source changes to it never trigger an auto-redeploy. The plan does not name autodeploy.sh as an edit target or acknowledge that the new service falls outside autodeploy's v1 surface.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` EDIT; `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` NEW
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` to `BOT_PATHS` in autodeploy.sh and change the compose up call to rebuild/restart both `review-bot` and `autolander` when either changes. If autolander is intentionally outside v1 auto-deploy, add an explicit `DETECT-ONLY` comment in the plan mirroring the CONFIG_PATHS pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the `willfarrell/autoheal` image to a specific tag (e.g. `v0.9.0`) and verify the label key that tag expects — either use `autoheal.enable=true` with the pinned tag, or explicitly set `AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL=autoheal` in the sidecar's environment to preserve the `autoheal=true` key.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the container label `autoheal=true` for the autolander service, but `willfarrell/autoheal` v0.9+ changed its default watched label to `autoheal.enable=true` (the old `autoheal=true` key is no longer matched by default unless `AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL=autoheal` is set). The plan neither pins the autoheal image tag nor verifies which label key the chosen tag expects, leaving a silent mismatch risk.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`: 'labeled `autoheal=true`, per S5d'
findings.suggested_fix: Pin to `willfarrell/autoheal:v0.9.0` (or latest confirmed tag) and use `autoheal.enable=true` as the container label, OR add `AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL: autoheal` to the autoheal service environment block to keep the `autoheal=true` label on the autolander.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the base image and runtime version for `Dockerfile.autolander` (e.g. `python:3.12-slim`) so the executor has no ambiguous design choice.
findings.finding: The Dockerfile base image is unspecified — the plan says 'installs the stdlib loop' but never names the runtime (Python version, Alpine, Debian-slim, distroless, etc.). An executor must make a real design choice that affects image size, CVE surface, and whether the loop's dependencies are satisfiable.
findings.location: Files section — `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files bullet: '`FROM python:3.12-slim` (or the project-standard base image); pin the version in the Dockerfile ARG.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the flock-as-sole-instance-guard decision (and the explicit rejection of `deploy.replicas: 1`) in a durable artifact — an ADR, a comment block in `docker-compose.yml`, or a `CLAUDE.md` / design-doc update — so the rationale survives beyond this ticket.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel, explicit architectural decision — flock as the SOLE single-instance mechanism, with `deploy.replicas: 1` explicitly prohibited — but does not call for this decision to be recorded in any durable artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc). Future maintainers modifying the compose file may not know why `deploy.replicas: 1` is absent and may (re-)add it, silently breaking the invariant.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Single-instance guard bullet; Files section (docker-compose.yml)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an entry to `docs/adr/` (or inline as a comment in `docker-compose.yml` directly above the autolander service) explaining: (1) why `deploy.replicas: 1` is absent, (2) why flock on the state volume is used instead, and (3) what properties of the volume are required for flock to work correctly (i.e., POSIX advisory-lock support on the backing filesystem).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in the Dockerfile entrypoint or a wrapper script) that unexpected flock/lockfile errors (non-EAGAIN failures, missing mountpoint, permission denied) are surfaced with a distinct non-zero exit code and a logged error message, separate from the clean 'already running' exit path.
findings.finding: The plan specifies what happens in the happy path (second instance exits) and the expected success state, but does not specify error handling or observability for *unexpected* flock-acquisition failures at startup — e.g., the state volume not mounted, the backing filesystem not supporting POSIX advisory locks, or a permissions error on the lockfile. These are new I/O failure points introduced by this step with no surfacing mechanism defined here (observability is wholesale deferred to S5d).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Single-instance guard bullet; State volume bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the container entrypoint script, distinguish three outcomes: (1) lock acquired → continue; (2) lock not acquired (EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK) → log 'already running, exiting' and exit 0 or a dedicated code; (3) unexpected error (EACCES, ENOENT, EROFS, etc.) → log the error with context and exit with a distinct code (e.g., exit 2) so operators and autoheal can tell the difference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure (e.g., remove the `autolander` service block and revert `compose-up.sh`, then `docker compose up -d`) to the plan, including what happens to the external volume on rollback.
findings.finding: No explicit rollback path is documented for removing the autolander service from the production Gerrit compose stack.
findings.location: ## Files / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' section: 'To revert: remove the `autolander` service and `autoheal` sidecar from docker-compose.yml, remove the volume entry from compose-up.sh, and run `docker compose up -d`. The external volume is left in place (safe to delete manually with `docker volume rm <name>` once traffic confirms no recovery is needed).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (a) how a stale flock lockfile from a crashed container is handled on next startup (POSIX advisory locks are released on process death, but document this explicitly), and (b) a retirement/cleanup procedure for the external volume when the service is decommissioned.
findings.finding: The state volume has no defined RETIRE or cleanup path — it will accumulate stale files (stale lockfile, old heartbeat/sentinel/drain records) indefinitely across container recreations.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — State volume bullet
findings.suggested_fix: For (a): note explicitly that POSIX advisory flocks are automatically released by the kernel when the process holding them dies, so a crashed container does not leave a blocking stale lock — this should be stated so future maintainers don't add unnecessary cleanup logic. For (b): add a 'Decommission' note: 'Remove the service from compose, then `docker volume rm <name>` after confirming no recovery data is needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add the autoheal sidecar to the plan title / scope statement, or explicitly defer it to S5d (where its HEALTHCHECK dependency and acceptance test live) and remove it from S5a's docker-compose.yml changes.
findings.finding: The autoheal sidecar (willfarrell/autoheal with Docker socket mount) is introduced in this ticket, but the title only promises 'Container + compose service + flock single-instance + external state volume' — the sidecar is an additional compose service beyond what the title names. Because the sidecar mounts the Docker socket read-only and manages the lifecycle of other labeled services, it is an independently impactful change to the stack that existing compose service operators may not anticipate from the title alone.
findings.location: Files section — 'infra/compose/docker-compose.yml' / Title vs. body scope
findings.suggested_fix: Move the autoheal sidecar addition to S5d (where its HEALTHCHECK and end-to-end validation belong) and update S5a's Files section accordingly; or extend the title to 'S5a Container + compose service + flock single-instance + external state volume + autoheal sidecar' and add an AC that `docker compose config` shows the sidecar with a valid socket mount.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for flock in the AC (e.g., 'flock is chosen because it is process-lifetime-scoped — the kernel releases it automatically on crash/exit — requires no external dependency, and works in plain compose, systemd, or any future orchestrator without change').
findings.finding: The plan states what NOT to use (deploy.replicas:1) but provides no positive rationale for why flock was chosen as the single-instance mechanism — e.g., 'flock is held by the process, releases automatically on crash, requires no external dependency, and works identically in plain compose and any future orchestrator.' Without affirmative rationale an implementer cannot distinguish design intent from implementation convenience, and cannot evaluate whether a future change (e.g. migrating to Swarm) should change the mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Single-instance guard AC bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Single-instance guard AC bullet: 'flock is chosen because the kernel releases the advisory lock automatically when the holding process exits (crash-safe, no stale-lock problem), it requires no daemon or external dependency, and it is portable across plain compose, Swarm, and any future runtime without change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why `willfarrell/autoheal` is preferred over compose-native `restart: unless-stopped` + HEALTHCHECK (the pattern already used by gerrit and review-bot in this stack), or replace the sidecar with the native mechanism if no concrete advantage exists.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `willfarrell/autoheal` as a compose sidecar with a Docker socket mount, but does not consider the established alternative already used by every other service in the stack: compose-native `restart: unless-stopped` combined with a HEALTHCHECK. Docker's own restart policy (with `--restart-max-attempts` or simply `unless-stopped`) restarts a container whose HEALTHCHECK enters the `unhealthy` state via the `--health-retries`/`--health-interval` mechanism. The plan neither names this prior art nor explains why it is insufficient compared to the autoheal sidecar.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` / Acceptance Criteria (autoheal sidecar)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief justification in the plan (or in a compose comment / ADR) comparing the two: if the only benefit is faster restart on HEALTHCHECK failure vs. Docker's exponential backoff, state that explicitly; if `restart: unless-stopped` suffices, remove the sidecar and the Docker socket mount.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a teardown note to the plan (e.g. in the Files or AC section) specifying the manual `docker volume rm <autolander_volume_name>` command and any pre-conditions (drain the service first, ensure no flock holder is running) needed to cleanly decommission the autolander state volume.
findings.finding: The plan adds the autolander state named volume as `external: true` but provides no teardown / decommission path for it. Every other external volume in the stack (gerrit_git, gerrit_db, etc.) also lacks explicit teardown documentation, but those are long-lived Gerrit data stores. The autolander state volume holds operational files (lockfile, heartbeat, emergency-stop sentinel, drain recovery record) that an operator would need to remove cleanly if the autolander service is retired or the stack is rebuilt. Without a teardown note, an operator running `docker compose down -v` would be surprised that the volume persists, and manual cleanup steps are unspecified.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — 'State volume' bullet; ## Files — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` EDIT

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
